skill.name: firecrawl-web
skill.description: Use Firecrawl to fetch web pages as markdown, capture screenshots, extract structured data, search the web, and crawl documentation sites.
skill.summary: Access Firecrawl web scraping, search, screenshot, extraction, and documentation crawling tools from Claude Code or Codex.
skill.author: BexTuychiev

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged critical combinations of network, filesystem, environment access, and command examples. Review found no confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection, but the skill legitimately sends user-selected URLs to Firecrawl, loads API keys from .env files, and writes files to user-provided paths. Publish only with clear warnings about third-party processing, credential handling, and output path control.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Web Requests to User-Supplied URLs
medium_findings.description: The script accepts user-provided URLs and sends them to Firecrawl for scraping, screenshots, extraction, search, and crawling. This is expected for the skill, but it can disclose requested URLs and retrieved page content to a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network behavior is explicit in Firecrawl scrape, search, and crawl calls. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the risk is real but not evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Home Environment File Loaded for Firecrawl Credentials
medium_findings.description: The script loads environment variables from the current directory and the user home .env file. This supports API key configuration, but broad home .env loading can expose unrelated secrets to process dependencies.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dotenv calls and README API key setup are direct evidence of environment credential access. I found no evidence that the script intentionally exfiltrates unrelated secrets.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Output Paths Can Write Files
medium_findings.description: The screenshot and crawl commands can write files to paths provided by the caller. This is useful functionality, but it can overwrite files or create many output files if used carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write operations are explicit and use command-line output paths. The risk depends on caller behavior because there is no path validation or overwrite protection.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Blocks Misclassified as Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command findings are shell examples in README.md and SKILL.md. They are not executed automatically by the skill, but users should still review installation commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is inside Markdown code fences and usage instructions. No runtime code path executes those snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: Base64 Decode Used for Screenshot Data
low_findings.description: The base64 decode operation handles image data returned by Firecrawl screenshots. I found no evidence that decoded content is executed or used to hide program behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding code checks for image data and writes screenshot bytes. This is a normal media handling pattern, not code obfuscation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings appear to match unrelated text such as parser descriptions and Markdown content. No cryptographic operation or weak hash usage was found in the reviewed code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain argument parser setup, printed descriptions, and skill metadata. I found no hashing, encryption, or signature verification logic.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Examples and Service References
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs in documentation are example targets, repository links, or Firecrawl service references. They do not indicate hidden network destinations in the script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible examples or setup links. Runtime destinations are supplied by the user through command arguments.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Bash, Read, and Write Tool Permissions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash, Read, and Write access while documenting command-line workflows. This is common for script-backed skills, but it expands the consequences of misuse or prompt-driven mistakes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tools are declared in the skill metadata. The risk is contextual because the permissions support the intended wrapper script.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Access Combined with Credential Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: The script loads environment credentials and then uses a network SDK. This is necessary for Firecrawl, but users should understand that API credentials are available to the running process.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both behaviors are present in clear code paths. I found no evidence that environment values are printed or sent directly outside normal Firecrawl SDK authentication.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary File Output Without Path Guardrails
dangerous_patterns.description: Output paths are accepted directly from command-line arguments for screenshots and crawl exports. The implementation does not restrict paths to a workspace or warn before overwriting.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write locations come from user arguments and no validation is visible. This supports a medium risk assessment rather than a block.

content.user_title: Scrape and Analyze Web Pages with Firecrawl
content.value_statement: AI coding tools often need current web content, screenshots, and structured page data. This skill connects Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to Firecrawl for web scraping, search, extraction, and documentation crawling.
actual_capabilities: Fetch a web page and return clean markdown content.
Capture a page screenshot and optionally save it as a PNG file.
Extract structured data from a page using a JSON schema file.
Run Firecrawl web searches with a configurable result limit.
Crawl documentation sites and save page markdown to a directory.
limitations: Requires a Firecrawl API key and available Firecrawl credits.
Sends requested URLs and page processing to the Firecrawl service.
Does not validate output paths before writing screenshot or crawl files.
Extraction quality depends on page structure, schema quality, and Firecrawl results.

use_cases.title: Research Current Web Content
use_cases.description: Fetch readable markdown from pages so an assistant can summarize, compare, or cite current information.

use_cases.title: Extract Product or Listing Data
use_cases.description: Use a schema to collect fields such as titles, prices, features, and descriptions from public pages.

use_cases.title: Learn New Documentation Sites
use_cases.description: Crawl a documentation site and save markdown pages for review or implementation support.
use_cases.target_user: Developers using Claude Code or Codex

prompt_templates.title: Fetch Page Markdown
prompt_templates.prompt: Get clean markdown from this URL and summarize the main points: https://example.com

prompt_templates.title: Capture a Page Screenshot
prompt_templates.scenario: Visual review of a web page.
prompt_templates.prompt: Take a screenshot of this page and save it as page.png: https://example.com

prompt_templates.title: Extract Structured Fields
prompt_templates.scenario: Schema-based extraction from a product page.
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the title, price, and feature list from this product URL using a schema file I provide.

prompt_templates.title: Crawl Documentation for Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Crawl this documentation site with a limit of 30 pages, then identify the setup steps and key API concepts.

output_examples.input: Fetch markdown from a product page.
output_examples.output: A readable page summary with the main heading, product details, pricing text, and important page sections.

output_examples.input: Search the web for a recent framework release.
output: A short list of matching results.
Each result includes a title, URL, and brief description.
The assistant can then inspect selected pages.

output_examples.input: Crawl a documentation site and save pages.
output_examples.output: A local directory of numbered markdown pages plus a count of saved pages.

best_practices: Use clear page URLs and set crawl limits before running documentation crawls.
Keep Firecrawl API keys in a dedicated environment file with minimal unrelated secrets.
Write screenshots and crawl output to a temporary project directory first.
anti_patterns: Do not crawl large sites without a page limit and a clear purpose.
Do not send private, internal, or sensitive URLs to Firecrawl unless approved.
Do not use output paths that could overwrite important files.

faq.question: Does this skill require an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses Firecrawl and requires a Firecrawl API key.

faq.question: Can it browse current web pages?
faq.answer: Yes. It can fetch, search, screenshot, extract, and crawl pages through Firecrawl.

faq.question: Does it store screenshots or crawled pages?
faq.answer: Only when the user provides an output path or directory.

faq.question: Can it extract structured data?
faq.answer: Yes. It reads a local JSON schema file and asks Firecrawl to return matching structured data.

faq.question: Is it safe for private URLs?
faq.answer: Use caution. Requested URLs and page content may be processed by Firecrawl.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses network access, environment credentials, command examples, and file writes for legitimate Firecrawl workflows.
